Hi Everyone -
I thought I would contribute to the collective knowledge here with my journey busting using Rivea Corymbosa Seeds, AKA "RC Seeds", AKA Ololiuqui. I will keep a log of it all in the format I've shown below. First I will give some quick background. (To skip to just the busting log, scroll to the bottom).
I'm a 35 year old male, first started getting CH when I was sixteen, in 2001. Cycles are generally ~7 weeks, and have been getting further and further apart the older I get, with ~3 years between cycles. When I was a teen I did the pharma route, finding mixed results with Prednisone taper and Inderal. But once those stopped working, a neurologist with a bad attitude started trying other drugs on me, and I started reading about the experiences of other CHers on clusterheadaches.com and here on clusterbusters, I said "no thank you" to the pharma route.
2003 - 2010
Sloppily experimenting with busting, different vitamins, prednisone/Inderal etc. I was 18-25 during this time, and you know how people are at that age! I had mixed results with mushrooms, and also had very mixed results with the high! One of the most enjoyable nights in my entire life was on mushrooms, and it stopped the CH dead in its tracks. I also had some of the most intensely anxious times in my life, and it didn't stop the CH. I attempted RC Seeds during this time, but back then the recommended starting dose was very low. I think I started with 8 seeds! So, I found no impact from using them.
2010 - 2020
Started the Vitamin D3 regimen. The D3 brought the severity of the pain during attacks WAY down. I've never been able to fully control a cycle using just the D3 regimen, but it has helped tremendously. During this time I also started using oxygen - *properly*! I was prescribed oxygen as a teenager, but was using the wrong mask and didn't have enough air flow, so I concluded that it didn't work for me. This was a total game-changer, obviously. I also started realizing that when I eat, I get attacks. I started talking to Batch a lot during this time. We talked about the body's PH and how keeping the body more alkaline could help. So I started drinking more lemon water, making tweaks like that, that I think helped a little bit. I tried 5-MEO-DALT for two cycles during this time as well. The results were mixed and I did not like the idea of weighing out these tiny, tiny increments of a random chemical I purchased in a sketchy way on the internet and ingesting it.
Latest Cycle - Started Nov. 2020
This cycle has been different for a number of reasons: 1) It came faster than expected. Typically the pain free time between my cycles increases by 3 months each cycle. It is crazy how accurately it has followed it my entire life! 3 months between my first cycle and my second, then six months, then 9 months, etc. etc. I was all the way up to three years between cycles, and then this latest one came after 2.5 years. This was very disappointing for me. 2) It has lasted much longer than

I know this is not the
shroomery.com
or somewhere else you would usually find a “trip report”, but maybe my experience can help anyone else who wants to raise their dose, but is struggling with it.
I think many people here have zero interest in a psychedelic trip. I had never tried psychedelics until I got this headache, and I probably would have never tried it otherwise. That’s unfortunate, because I think it has had a positive impact on my mental health. Even if my headache condition were permanently cured tomorrow, I would still want to take a nice strong trip every now and then after having experienced it.
With a “trip report”, I see most recreational users try to exaggerate or hype up their experiences. The point of the story is make it sound crazy and exciting, or “ultra spiritual”. We aren’t looking for that while just trying to medicate ourselves. I don’t want or need a crazy experience or a spiritual revelation at this frequency.
I’ll try to do just the opposite, I’ll tell a story of how I keep heroic doses bland, pleasant, and safe.
First off, I never started small. I noted that shrooms are very physically safe, asked a friend what a “respectable” dose is, and went with it. I started with 5 grams of Albino Penis Envy. Yes, that friend is a little bit of an ass, but I’m grateful for the end results anyway.
It was a very strange and interesting day, full of bouts of euphoria punctuated with existential dread. I was closed in my bedroom, accompanied by my cat and two small dogs. I did not have a sitter. Nothing bad happened, everything went back to normal after it passed. I don’t recommend skipping the sitter to anyone else, you should at least have someone else in the house.
I didn’t really know much about the neuroscience behind psychedelics at that point. So I just took the ride at face value, with no preconceived ideas. Just an unshakable faith in my physical safety and that things would fall back together after each session.
My subsequent trips were similar, until I decided to break up a dose to stretch my supply. 2.5 grams put me in a terrible state of anxiety and nervous fear. I dreaded using the other 2.5 grams, but my supplier had ghosted me.
Now I know a little more about the neuroscience. I am sure that I feel more comfortable at the higher doses because it actually turns off the “Default Mode Network”, a series of structures in your brain that are primarily responsible for the “narrator” in your head. If your consciousness can be described as combination of spotlight, representing your attention/sensory experience, and an operator/narrator purposefully shining it around, then my consciousness became free of the operator. It was just shining around with no guidance, wondrously random and chaotic. The part of brain responsible for limiting and organizing sensory input also took a little break, and I experienced sight and sound like I had never before.

Hi everyone! I want to share my successful bust story and share what one should expect moving to or coming to Portugal with a CH.
A breif note on my case:
I have episodic CH
Last year, my doctor in Turkey prescribed me Cymbalta (Duloxetine, an SNRI) as an antidepressant. I am still taking 60 mg daily
I am a 6’6”, 200-pound male (which may or may not be relevant to the RC dose and side effects)
I moved to Portugal in early February, and my cycle started in mid-February
I hadn't had any busting experience before
CH treatment in Portugal:
You must have a prescription from a local doctor to get triptans. I assume a prescription from any doctor in Europe would be accepted, but mine was in Turkish and wasn’t accepted
In mid-February, the earliest available appointment with a neurologist in Porto or Braga was in mid-March. I went to a family doctor who gave me a prescription, but he could only prescribe a small number of pills, like high single digit. To get a sufficient supply for a full cycle, you need to see a neurologist
I was lucky enough to find an appointment in late February in a small town in northern Portugal. The doctor was quite knowledgeable and prescribed me an entire warchest of triptans. I think I now have enough for 2–3 bad cycles
Oxygen tanks are not easy to obtain here. You must be registered in the Portuguese healthcare system, and only a pulmonologist can prescribe them to you. The neurologist tried to get me prescription without me going to a pulmonologist, but ultimately failed to do so.
By the time I visited the neurologist, I had already ordered RC seeds. So, I decided to postpone jumping through all those hoops until later to prepare for the next cycle
My busting story:
I ordered RC seeds from
http://dutch-headshop.be
.
Thank you
@Shaun brearley
for sharing this website in another thread. I am sorry if it was originally posted by anyone else
These guys offer various seeds and mushrooms, but you can’t order the latter to Portugal since mushrooms are illegal here. You can still order the seeds, spores, and growing kits, though.
Keep in mind that the website only accepts bank transfers, which means you need to allow 2–3 days for the payment to be processed. I received my seeds in 8 days—3 days for payment processing and 5 days for delivery.
The first treatment:
I waited 5 days after taking my last triptan. I was lucky enough to have relatively mild attacks (as far as CH goes), so I managed without triptans and oxygen
I followed
@MoxieGirl
's recipe you can find here
https://clusterbusters.org/forums/topic/2353-moxiegirls-seed-recipe/
On Monday, I took one package of RC seeds, which contained 40 seeds. After doing the floating test, I found only 3 bad seeds. I then ground, soaked, and drank the concoction made from the remaining 37 seeds.
I didn't have a trip or any side effects whatsoever.
On Tuesday, I had a slapback. It wasn't a full-blown attack, so it was manageable without taking a painkiller or oxygen, however, it was sharp

To my wonderful Cluster Buster Community,
I want to share my success story with busting thanks to the help and advice of many fellow cluster heads, specifically
@xxx
,
@CHfather
,
@Bejeeber
, and
@Shaun brearley
, who took the time to answer my many questions and to guide me in my journey.  Thanks to all of you, I asked my Functional Medicine doctor what she thought of the busting idea (she is an alternative doc who left Mayo to go out on her own), and she referred me to a patient of hers who could provide me with mushrooms. It involved crossing state lines into Michigan from Wisconsin and a 7.5-hour drive each way, but it was well worth it. I only got halfway before I had to stop and get a hotel so I could use my oxygen and get off the road for the 6 attacks I knew I would have before morning. I was able to make it back the next afternoon before the attacks began again. I waited another week to get all of the meds out of my system to do my first bust. I did .4 grams the first time. I could not believe how great I felt. I would not say I was hallucinating but what I didn't expect was that the mushrooms would take away my shadows and general low-grade pain I had between attacks. I felt amazing. I had forgotten what it felt like to not be in pain. I was so relieved I was in tears.
The first night I had 6 attacks, but each night after that, they became less severe and fewer. After the one-week waiting period, I did another bust with .6 grams. Again I felt great. From there, I gradually improved, and within a few days, my shadows were gone, and I was done with my cycle! It felt like a miracle, and I never even had to do a third bust. That was almost 3 months ago, and I am still cluster-free.
I was also able to get extra mushrooms which are dried and frozen, so I know that if a cycle returns, I have what I need to heal myself. That knowledge has made me much less anxious and makes this condition less of a tragedy for me.
I would never have had the knowledge or confidence to do a bust had it not been for this trusted group of amazing people! Thank you from the bottom of my heart. This group is saving lives and is literally the only resource that is truly knowledgable and willing to share their personal experiences and information.
I truly hope that there is legal access to hallucinogenics soon and that the medical community accepts this as a legitimate treatment.
You are my heroes!

My name is Brendan Burns and I have been a cluster sufferer for just about 20 years now.  Today I am here to talk about the research I have come across and the experiences that I have had over the past 6 years treating my headaches with the dissociative hallucinogen, Ketamine Hydrochloride.
First off I figured I would tell you a bit about myself and my situation.  I had my first cluster when I was 17.  I was lucky enough to be correctly diagnosed within the first 4
years and like most people I was prescribed triptan medications. For a while this worked.  Eventually however, the clusters were getting worse and worse and I was needing a constant supply of triptans.  Not long after I had turned 24 a bit of strangeness stepped in--I started having grand mal seizures due to an undiagnosed gluten allergy.  At this time my clusters started getting much worse but now I was also experiencing other headaches that did not always fit the typical cluster headache profile.  In particular many of them would last longer and act more like a migraine, some sort of other TAC or sometimes a severe tension headache.
Eventually I overcame the seizures by changing my diet but the headaches have remained.  I mention all this because although I have been diagnosed with cluster headaches, I also seem to have some other sort of headache that can mimic cluster at times.   Early on it was hard for me to be able to tell the difference but these days I feel I have a handle on what is what a bit better.

What sets the HP Gland to go off its regular schedule from 1x a day?
Barometric Pressure from one of the following  causes but not limited to:
Change in season - lengthening of days Spring to Summer, Summer to Fall, Fall to Winter
Geographical location - by the coast you get lots of low/high pressure changes from storms or marine layer or fog
Weather changes - low pressure from storms (high pressure is usually good as it is stable)
Stress - adds up to pressure in the head which affects the HP
irregular sleeping patterns
stimulants
Why is it important to keep it on a regular schedule?
When it goes off from the body's clock, trouble starts for CH people.
For example let's say I have four CH per day like clockwork at
11am
4pm
11pm
4am
this is HP (Hypothalamus Gland) triggering at the wrong time
Here's what I think is going on...
At 11am the HP is triggered for the second time today. It is off the from the body's clock. They should be in sync going off at the same time. This would be my normal waking time.  We all have one natural rising time, but two different alarms in the form of an HP clock and Body clock. Hopefully they go off once a day, together in sync.
My HP clock is operating too fast
I think that as opposed to a trigger, the headache has come about because the HP clock has been "Speed Up".  The change in seasons confused it and now it is running to fast. HP clock thinks a day is only six hours long instead of 24. It is moving too fast.
Now the HP gland is triggering at 11 rather than at my natural time is because it is off.  In my case it is already on the second alarm in a single day! My body is getting the message to wake up, its the start of the day again. The rest of the body realizes it is already almost mid day. The body clock is working fine. It reacts to the message and says "terrorist" and sends its defenses to attack. Instantly the histamines flow and everything gets worse now. Chemical imbalances in the body. Pressure builds in your sinuses. This sinus pressure puts pressure on and triggers the TG nerve. Now the pain receptors have been flooded by a "key " molecule that fits into them. This molecule we will call K. It is the molecule that fits in the receptor causing a protein P to be released in the brain saying "OW! HOLY HELL THIS HURTS!"
Your body clock does not quit fighting. It continues to send messages and fight the imaginary terrorist trying to wake up the body for no reason.
Your brain is flooded by K everywhere. all your pain receptors are being blown up and sucked out of the protein P.
You feel the headache as long as K is filled and triggering P to your brain.
The good news is P runs out. That is when your headache stops. Some people hold a lot of P some hold a little. This is called tolerance of Pain. you have a  lot of P you will suffer a lot of pain. Hello, Cluster Busters!  I am the one they call Onglamesh, and I've been a skull crushing, writhing crybaby for 10 years.  This is my first time posting publicly about my most effective form of relief... I fear possible repercussions for coming out and saying what I'm going to say, but my ever increasing contemplation of the term "suicide headache" has convinced me that I
MUST
share this information... what if somebody on the brink of ending it all happens upon this and finds new hope?  I've decided that possibility is FAR more significant to me than the negative... if I get busted for busting, due to talking about how I do it, then so be it... at least I can feel like I may have helped somebody abandon the thought of taking their own life.  Because I can honestly say that I've thought about it more than enough times, and I may have done it if I hadn't stumbled upon such exquisite relief.
I learned about cluster headache only 2 years into my struggle.  I also learned immediately that all the medications offered by doctors are very pitifully ineffective for most of us, and that the majority of sufferers seek medical help but continue to suffer because the medical world gives less than a shit about us.  I learned that most doctors don't even know what CH is, and will misdiagnose you or refer you to a dozen specialists who also don't know what it is and send you on the scavenger hunt for ineffective drugs with really bad side effects.  So I must confess, I've never been to see a doctor about my condition, I gave up on that idea before trying... and for that, I thank all you brave souls who have been running the medical gauntlet and sharing your experiences with newcomers like me.
I've taken acetaminophen, ibuprofen, and naproxen as pathetic excuses for relief over the years.  I would take whichever one was handy, or combine the acetaminophen with one of the NSAIDs, knowing that it will barely do anything if anything at all... but rationalizing that it's better than doing nothing to try and stop the pain.  I've been given darvocet, morphine, dilaudid, hydrocodone, and probably various other pain meds when somebody has one to spare.  None of them took my pain away; if I'm lucky, they'd get me a little bit high so that I care a little bit less.  So I gave up on prescription drugs 7 years ago and just took the OTCs with little to no benefit.  I went through a period of alcoholism (now sober for 5 years) not caused by the headache, but certainly utilized at times to help me dissociate from it.  Sure, alcohol triggers the headache, but if you drink yourself into oblivion, you won't even remember that you had one.  The problem with that method is you still wake up feeling like the hot pokers are rigged up to a jackhammer.

Drug developers are designing new psychedelic compounds to
treat depression and other mental-health conditions
but skip the trip.
Mind-bending psychedelics including MDMA (aka “ecstasy”), “magic mushrooms” and LSD are being studied as potential
treatments for depression, post-traumatic stress disorder and addiction
. Dozens of companies and academic laboratories are also making changes to the structure of those drugs, or designing similar compounds, to take advantage of their therapeutic properties without the high.
Human research on the newly developed compounds is just getting started. Some researchers and analysts said the trip itself could be critical to the therapeutic benefit for patients.
Drugs including psilocybin and mescaline aren’t patentable because they occur naturally in mushrooms or plants. Others including LSD and MDMA were created in labs decades ago.
Global revenue from psychedelics
could reach $8 billion by 2027, L.E.K. Consulting estimated.
Psychedelic drugs are tightly regulated.
Some including MDMA impart effects that last for hours and require patient supervision during medical use.
Companies and researchers said creating new compounds with shorter-acting or non-hallucinogenic effects could lead to fewer restrictions, and make them easier for doctors and patients to use.
New drugs could also open their use to people who are currently excluded from psychedelic treatment for safety reasons, including a history of schizophrenia or some heart conditions, they said.
“We have to create medicines that are safe enough that people can take them at home and put them in their medicine cabinet,” said
David Olson, chief innovation officer at Delix Therapeutics, which is developing drugs similar to substances such as LSD and psilocybin but without the trip. The company said it raised some $100 million and plans to move two drugs into human trials next year.

I know my case will be anecdotal but I still wanted to share my experience in the hope that it will help others suffering from cluster headaches. I first started experiencing cluster headaches at age 21 directly following the death of both my sisters and my father in a commercial plane crash. The trauma from that is impossible to describe, but I believe it was a trigger that lead me to having cluster headaches. There hasn't been a lot of research between trauma and chronic pain conditions, but I believe that the cluster headaches were a physical manifestation of intense psychological pain.
I also started drinking lots of alcohol to cope with the loss I had experienced and I believe this was a huge trigger for cluster headaches. College became almost impossible for me after that even though I still managed to graduate. Being forced to leave class or work because your head is about to explode makes cluster headaches more than painful, it makes them debilitating. I luckily had it good compared to other accounts I have read on here. I would get cluster headaches for about 3 months out of the year with attacks happening around 3-4 times daily. This went on for the next 15 years.
The last time I had a cluster headache attack was 2 years ago and this is the longest I have ever been in remission. So what cured my headaches? Psilocybin mushrooms. When I was around 23 years old I went on the website Erowid.com to try and see if there was anecdotal evidence of illicit drugs helping my condition. I read post after post of people using DMT, LSD and psilocybin to cure cluster headaches so I tried them all. DMT was the most effective for being able to immediately abort a headache in around 10 minutes. Because DMT is smoked and not eaten it has a much faster activation time and literally saved my life. For the last 13 years every time I have a cluster headache attack I smoke DMT out of a vaporizer pen and the relief is almost immediate.
I ran into a problem the last time I had a cluster headache episode. The DMT was not working. This was a serious problem and I went into full blown panic mode. I contacted some friends and was able to get my hands on a literal pound of psilocybin mushrooms. The next time I had a cluster headache I started eating mushrooms and didn't stop until the pain went away. I was eating around 2 grams of mushrooms at a time every 4 hours or so but it was just barely making the headache subside. So I just kept eating more and more and more. After I had consumed around an 28 grams of mushrooms I completely lost mind. The pain also wasn't completely gone, so something in me forced me to just keep eating more mushrooms. Over the course of the next 2 days I ate a half pound of psilocybin mushrooms. I know that dosage sounds really hard to believe, but if you know about cluster headaches you will do ANYTHING to make it stop. I was then basically schizophrenic for the next 2 weeks. Apologies for keeping you in suspense about my experience last Saturday with shrooms
@CHfather
In summary, this second occasion was a very good experience and completely different from the first. Very peaceful, very healing and with a lot of learning.
I took 1.5 grams of Blue Minies, which seem to have an above-average intensity compared to other strains of cubensis mushrooms, I was told. The first time take 1 gram of them.
This time I started with a panic attack, below I explain why it was, but it lasted only about 40 minutes and then I managed to control it with meditation and breathing. It also helped to know that I had the Xanax here with me, although I didn't have to use it. I also have a sitter onsite and a few other available remotely via zoom.
When I was able to calm down, I understood that I had full control of the situation, that I would not be able to harm myself, or harm anyone, and that the effect of the mushrooms was completely controllable. I will be 62 this year. I have lived with cluster headaches since 1970. I remember my first episode occurred in 10th grad Spanish class. I had zero idea what was happening all I know is I had to get out of class fast and hide..somewhere. I had no idea this was to be the start of a lifelong journey through the the worst pain I have ever endured. As the episodes would start it became harder to hide them and of course my family had me tested for all the underlying cause and brain tumor was ruled out. So since no doctor could conclude what was causing my headache I pretty much gave up and decided that as long as they would eventual disappear I would just deal with it.
Over the years I got pretty good at figuring out the timing or the onset . Yes they were definitely cyclical, seasonal. In my 's 30's and 40's I could count on once a year for a period of 6-7 weeks I would have to deal with the pain.
I also became pretty good at hiding the pain. It's unfortunately a pretty misunderstood disorder and because of that there is a certain amount of shame involved, after all it's just a headache , we all get them.
The fear is that they will occur when you can't disappear and work though the pain. Like the first date with my wife of 38 years. At a drive in to see Jaws. It's a wonder she ,married me. God you feel like a jerk writhing in pain eye watering and then exhaustion when it finally eases up.
Anyhow writing this is my way of finally deciding not to hide and ride them out. Fortunately I am in a position with my work that as the boss I can disappear if needed and not too many people will question it. So when the need arises I will find a car in the lot out of the way and settle in for an hour of hurt then collect myself and resume my day.
I the thing I can't control is being in public places or events or parties or dinner with friends when I have no escape route. No place to hide. So for the cluster period I choose  not to put myself in that position and my wife lives the same life until they pass.

Of course an intrigue is whether the holotropic breathwork that purportedly induces full on psychedelic states could also possibly serve as some sort of busting technique?
Breathing Their Way to an Altered State
As psychedelics move from the underground to mainstream medicine, clinicians aspiring to work in the field are inducing altered states with deep breathing.
By
Ernesto Londoño
Reporting from San Francisco
Published
Jan. 9, 2024
Updated
Jan. 10, 2024
The instructions were simple: Lying on cots while wearing eyeshades, participants were directed to take deep belly breaths without pause to the beat of fast-paced music booming from loudspeakers.
The exercise, they were told, had the potential to induce an altered state of consciousness so profound that breathers sometimes describe it as reliving the terrifying moment of their birth. Past participants claim to have caught glimpses of past lives.
A few minutes into the session, which lasted nearly three hours, several participants began to weep. Some shook their limbs wildly, looking possessed. An outsider walking in would have been startled by the scene.
But the dozens of attendees at the recent breathwork workshop in San Francisco were far from hippies or cult initiates. They were health care professionals completing the final step of a certificate program in psychedelic therapy.
The vigorous modality, known as holotropic breathwork, is offered at the end of an eight-month training to provide a lawful taste of the therapeutic potential and pitfalls of altered states of consciousness.
Dr. JJ Pursell, a naturopathic doctor from Oregon, was among the trainees who walked into the early October session skeptical that a couple of hours of intense breathing could induce anything close to a psychedelic trip. But she was stunned.
“The depth of what I experienced was so similar to psilocybin,” Dr. Pursell marveled, referring to the psychoactive compound in magic mushrooms. “It was trippy.”
Image
Students in a San Francisco breathwork seminar wept and shook their limbs. “The depth of what I experienced was so similar to psilocybin,” Dr. JJ Pursell recalled.
Credit...
Damien Maloney for The New York Times
A Promising Field of Study, Long Fallow
The demand for psychedelics as tools for healing has exploded in recent years, fueled by promising clinical studies, loosening drug laws and a growing number of celebrities who have come out as psychonauts. The N.F.L. star Aaron Rodgers described ayahuasca ceremonies as a catalyst for “
deeper self love
.” In her new autobiography, the actor
Jada Pinkett Smith
credited ayahuasca sessions with putting an end to her suicidal ideation. And ketamine, an anesthetic that induces a dissociative, psychedelic-like state, has become an
increasingly popular treatment
for depression among people who get it at clinics or self-medicate.

I thought I'd take a moment and pen my whole
Cluster Headache
story (as briefly as I can). I find I post all, or part of it, in a number of new member posts quite frequently when people ask if
Busting
really works, and if there is any hope out there to be had, so thought I'd post it here, then I can just drop a link to it in posts. Lazy, I know.
Migraines
run in my family. I've had them all my life, all my siblings have them, my nieces and nephews and my 2 children. They have just always been a part of our family. My triggers are normally: stress, being over tired, hunger, thirst, changes in weather, sudden cold, strong smells, bright lights, and probably a few other things. I would normally go a year or so with none, or very few migraines, then be in a stressful situation and have a bunch.
In 2006/2007 I transitioned from male to female. In 2006 I started on a high dose of Oestrogen and an Anti-androgen designed to shut off my Testosterone. On 31 Dec 2006, at around 10pm, I got a headache. I wasn't ringing in the new year with friends that year. As chance would have it, I was home alone and not drinking. Just having a quiet night to myself. So the headache wasn't a result of alcohol or loud music or anything. Just a headache.
That headache lasted for
7 months,
and finally started breaking up in July when I'd got a few days here and there pain free. Then it got even better in August after I flew to Thailand for surgery. This was diagnosed as
Chronic Daily Headaches
(although at the time, it was in fact one non-stop headache).
That very first week of 2007 was shocking! On top of this headache, I got a bad
Migraine
and my first ever
Cluster Headache
. At that time in my life, my migraines were lasting 2-7 days, 3-4 days being the average. The
Cluster Headache
totally blew me away. I had no idea what it was.
I started out getting 2-5 clusters a week. I would have one Tuesday night at either 7pm or 7:30pm, another one or two on Thursdays, the occasional Wednesday and one or two over the weekend. That was pretty regular my first year. Plus, of course, nearly weekly migraines that were lasting days.
2007 was my Year of Hell. It was, quite literally, just variations in the level of pain for 7 months.
Thankfully, my GP had a good friend who had clusters, so she correctly diagnosed them on my first visit and got me straight to a neurologist. The neurologist said he had come across a number of transsexuals who got clusters. He believed that my body was predisposed to having them, and the change in my hormones just triggered it. Once triggered, there was no going back, unfortunately. It's pretty accepted that clusters are linked to the Hypothalamus, and also pretty accepted that changing one's hormones alters the Hypothalamus.

I have battled CH on and off for 13 years
it was only this past year that I realized cluster headaches are in fact a real thing and are in fact what's wrong with me. I have been trying to help myself for so long. treating with every kind of over the counter medication you can think of. always with no success. no real reliable relief. finally this year during my spring cycle things got so bad I wound up in the ER. more than once.  I never would have imagined clusters could do that to me.
that's what it took for me to realize what this was. all my life I thought I was sort of over reacting or being weak about something normal people go through with little trouble. as a kid in school I just had to put my head down and bare it. occasionally I would get some time in the clinic or sent home early. growing up it has always caused trouble with work. I don't like to look like a complainer or weak or someone who makes excuses. so I have always kept my mouth shut and soldiered through what I had to. and when I couldn't bare it, quietly submit to the pain at home before passing out on over the counter pain meds. anything to stop the pain.
I have finally come to terms with living with ch. with that I hope I can be of some help to others who may need it
There are 3 key things I have found to help me through an attack or a cycle
STAY CALM!
I have found that panic can make the pain more severe and during a more severe attack, especially if a remedy fails you, you are more likely to panic. this can quickly become a vicious cycle. stay calm soldier on.
OXYGEN!
high flow oxygen usually counters an attack in about 20 minutes if I catch it at the right time. since Ive accepted my diagnosis from a neurologist I was lucky enough to get oxygen prescribed to me from a doctor at a free clinic. I did this without any insurance and was able to find a local oxygen vendor who also deals with the general public.
MAGIC MUSHROOMS
these are last on the list for a reason. that reason mainly being their legal status. make no mistake these things will change your life. obviously not for kids and use them at your own discretion. that being said I have found incredible relief dosing with MM. sometimes short lived but still better than anything over the counter.
in the past before I knew what I was going through I would try anything and everything to combat an attack. I had a few little rituals and tricks but the main goal was usually to get home or  to a dark room so I could try to relax an eventually go to sleep.
If none of those 3 key options work for you these are a few things I have used in the past to help find relief
caffeine
- this was always a go to when I needed to buy some time. chugging an energy drink soda or coffee always saved me for a few hours
bengay
- in recent years I sometimes use bengay or similar topical ointment. rubbing it on liberally over my shoulders neck and even my face, around my temples and sinus'. this helped to relax and relieve some nasal pressure

Hi everybody. I have had New Daily Persistant Headache (NDPH) for the last decade. My biggest symptoms are headache (right-sided), anxiety attack, and brain fog, and I use none of these terms casually.
Only recently have I tried to alleviate my symptoms with magic mushrooms. Results thus far are confusing and positive at the same time. Tell me what you think.
First Dose
- roughly 3 grams dry, without fasting beforehand, taken in the evening...
That night, I felt no psychedelic effect or body sensation whatsoever from the mushrooms, and no change in my symptoms. I thought this was unusual, considering the dose. But the next day, my symptoms were slightly less intense. I didn't know what to make of it. Was it a coincidence? It's surprising to think that a high dose of mushrooms could have no noticeable effects the night of consumption, but have a positive benefit the next day.
Second Dose
- seven days later and roughly 4 grams dry, fasting beforehand, taken in the evening...
I felt a
very
mild body high, but no psychedelic effect at all. I was confused again by the lack of sensation, but as before, the following morning my NPDH symptoms were a bit milder than usual. Now I didn't think it was a coincidence.
Third Dose
- five days later and roughly 4 grams dry via
lemon tek
, fasting beforehand, taken in the morning...
I dosed in the morning so I could analyze the experience throughout the day. I am glad I did this, because the day was a tale of two halves. The first half was unpleasant. Again no psychedelic effect from the mushrooms. I did feel a body sensation, but to be honest, it was a body "low" rather than a body "high". It felt like the weakness that accompanies a mild flu. I also felt depressed and my stomach was slightly upset, so it really did just feel like I was sedated and under the weather. I was wondering how anyone could consider it fun. Then the experience changed. The uncomfortable sensations passed as my body stabilized, and strangely enough, I felt peaceful, as in a state of afterglow that would normally follow a psychedelic trip, but for me just happened to follow a few hours of unpleasantness. This lasted the rest of the day. In this afterglow, my NDPH symptoms were uncharacteristically mild. My headache and anxiety where the lowest they've been in many months, and my brain fog wasn't as worrisome at it normally is either. No, my disease did not miraculously disappear, but it unquestionably responded. The experience makes me optimistic about future doses of mushrooms. I still don't know why I didn't experienced anything close to the psychedelic fun that recreational users rave about, but I don't really care about that anyway. If I can reproduce and extend the relief that arose in the afterglow, then it will be worth it.

I was just chatting with a member of the forum in PM about how I put my MM into capsules and take 'em that way as I hate the taste of mushrooms. It was suggested I make a post with info on how to do this, so, here I am.
I use
The Capsule Machine
. This is a UK link, but you should be able to get it anywhere.
Capsules come in 3 sizes: "00", "0" and "1". Size "00" is the largest, and size "1" is the smallest. (
No, it doesn't make any sense to me either.
)
I purchased size "0", which is about the same size as a capsule of cold medicine you'd buy over the counter. Obviously, the larger capsule the fewer pills you have to swallow.
The size is important, because when you order your capsules, you need to get the right size capsule for the machine you have. I ordered 1,000 capsules from Amazon for £10 or so. You can get the machine with 500 capsules if you look around a bit.
It's very important that you get your shrooms cracker dry, and I do mean CRACKER dry before doing this. Some people put them in the oven on a low heat, I tend to leave mine over a warm radiator. Whatever works, get them dry.
I then crush them in a Mortar & Pestle. I've tried a couple different ways, but this proves to be the most consistent method to get them really crushed. I try to get them almost to the point of powder.
You then separate the capsules and load up the machine, it's pretty obvious which end goes where. Be sure the capsule halves are pushed ALL the way down into the holes. Pour some crushed shroom into the larger part of the machine and use the tapper to tap down the shroom powder. Add more, tap again, add more tap again.
Once you press the two halves back together, and eject the completed capsule, be sure to press them together just a bit more. I've found they don't completely lock into place straight from the machine.
It's been awhile since I've made any, and I'm trying to recall how much I put in each capsule. I normally bust with 1.2 grams, and that works out to 4-5 capsules. You can obviously pack them light, and not put much in if you want to micro-dose, or load them up as much as you can.
What did was put 10 empty capsules on a scale and weighted them, and divided by 10 to get the average weight of an empty capsule. Then, load them up and weigh them, being sure to use the same 10 capsules. Divide by 10, and you'll get the average weight each capsule holds of shrooms.
A moderate crop can easily produce 50+ capsules. I put them in an empty pill bottle with a Desiccant bag to keep them dry, then store in the fridge.
For me, it completely transformed my busting experience. I dreaded the taste of mushroom tea, in fact, I can't even smell peppermint tea anymore without wanting to be sick. It also means I know exactly how much I'm taking and there's no fuss.
If my next crop is successful (fingers crossed) I'll add to this post and include some pictures.

I'm a 50 y/o Dutch guy and each spring and fall I suffer from CH.

I have been visiting this forum for some time now and I noticed that a lot of people seek practical information about using or obtaining shrooms or truffles from Holland.
With this post I will try and give those who seek information about the subject some quick answers and hopefully you'll find them usefull.
All the information below is factual and/or based on personel experience.
You can read it as an rough guide.
If you need more information or help in any way just ask or send me a PM.
Is it legal to buy Magic Mushrooms in Holland?
No.
Since 2008 it is prohibited to buy or sell Magic Mushrooms.
Is it legal to buy mushroom spores or growboxes in Holland?
Yes.
To grow your own mushrooms you can either buy spores or just simple buy a complete growbox.
A growbox is a complete set with instructions and really easy to do.
Spores and/or growbox are legal to be sold and possessed by people of 18 years and older in the Netherlands by Dutch law.
Is it legal to buy truffles in Holland?
Yes.
Since the goverment introduced a ban on mushrooms they forgot to include truffles.
Truffles are legal to be sold and possessed by people of 18 years and older in the Netherlands by Dutch law.
Where can I buy truffles, spores or growboxes?
There are a lot of so called smartshops where you can buyÂ  truffles, spores or growboxes.
You will find a smartshop in almost every major Dutch city or on-line.
What is the best smartshop to visit?
All smartshops buy their spores, growboxes or truffles from the same growers/wholesalers.
It all comes down to freshness of the product. The shop with the biggest turn-over usually sells the best quality.
Can I mail order truffles, spores or growboxes?
Most web-based smartshops will send you your order but with a disclaimer stating that you are responsible for informing yourself on the legal status, import and custom laws of these products in your country.
Is there a difference between truffles and mushrooms?
For aborting or trying to bust CH there is no difference.
Both truffles and mushrooms contain psiclocybine in a more or less same amount and the effects are similar.
Is there a difference between the various truffel products offered?
Smartshops sell truffles with all kinds of fancy names and with al kinds of alleged specific properties. Mostly this is just marketing.
For mushrooms there are different species and some are believed to be more potent then others.
From a botanical point of view this is true but remember truffles and mushrooms are a product of nature. The conditions of growing, the exact time of picking and freshness have an huge impact on the individual potency.
For aborting or trying to bust CH ANY magic mushroom or truffle will do.

The major alkaloids involved are Harmine, Harmaline, and Tetrahydroharmine.Â  These are all Beta Carbolines, They all inhibit the MAO A enzyme, and they all are considered RIMA's--Reversible Inhibitors of MAO subtype A.Â (For more info on RIMA's you can look into the pharmaceutical anti-depressant moclobomide, which has MAO A enzyme as it's mechanism of action.)Â  Â All 3 substances are completely legal in the U.S., as are both the Caapi Vine and Rue seeds.Â  I know I have read that they are hitting some of the same receptors as Psilocybin and LSA, but right now I can't find my sources.

So no risk of "hypertensive crisis"Â  Just forget about it.Â  There is the theory that if you are on RIMA's and eat lots and lots of tyramine containing foods that eventually you could "overwhelm" the system and cause this crisis, but I have NEVER heard of this actually happening.Â  From what I've read it seems more like people have been scared of MAO inhibitors for a long time and are reluctant to let go of that fear.Â  One of the biggest sources of tyramine is chees

https://www.healtheuropa.eu/worlds-first-magic-mushroom-nasal-spray-for-ptsd-and-depression/95434/
PTSD and depression are two conditions that are being trialled for treatment with psilocybin.
A ‘magic mushroom’ nasal spray has been designed to make microdosing easier for people trying to treat their PTSD or depression.
Silo Wellness, an Oregon company with a team with roots in cannabis delivery modalities, has developed a magic mushroom nasal spray in Jamaica – where psilocybin is legal – for controlled, metered-dosing known as ‘microdosing’.
Magic mushroom nasal spray
The psilocybin nasal spray passes the gut, going directly to the bloodstream through the nasal mucus membranes and eventually the liver for metabolising. This saves the patient from feeling nauseous, as commonly experienced when taking psilocybin.
Board Advisor and Silo Wellness investor Becky Rotterman, a Missouri pharmacist, stated: “Many psilocybin patients, particularly women, complain of upset stomach or vomiting when taking high-doses of mushrooms.
“We want to bring this wonderful natural medicine first to Oregon and then the flyover states – to those who would be afraid to eat a handful of fungi and who feel more comfortable seeing their medicine in a familiar delivery modality, such as a metered-dose nasal spray.”
The correct dose
When a patient takes psilocybin the dosage is vital. Many people end up ‘stacking’, whereby the first dose takes some time to take effect so the patient consumes more, only to find they have taken too much later on. Microdosing alternatives with faster uptake speed are important  in order to prevent accidental high-dosage experiences.
Silo Wellness founder, Mike Arnold, said he created the medicine dispenser after his first experience with psilocybin after he was advised by a doctor to try it.
He said: “I couldn’t help to think how fortunate I was to have an actual physician take me on my first trip rather than ‘some guy’ I know, but I realised that most first-time users don’t have that luxury.
“I reached out to my long-time colleague from the marijuana space, brilliant inventor Michael Hartman, and we agreed that we need to be able to give patients predictable dosing so they can self-titrate into the desired levels of sub-psychedelic or psychedelic treatment.”
“We solved the age-old problem with plant- and fungus-based medicine: How do you know how much is a dose?” Hartman explained. “How do you avoid taking too much, like the cannabis edibles dilemma? We also managed to solve one of the common complaints of some mushroom users: taste and upset stomach.”
Healing trauma with magic mushrooms
Psilocybin is being trialled for the treatment of a number of physical and mental conditions, two of which are PTSD and depression. The psychoactive component is thought to stimulate receptors in the brain and potentially ‘rewire the brain’ by increasing neuro connectivity – helping to heal trauma from physical or psychological events.
COO, Scott

I’m 56 years old, and I suffer from episodic cluster headaches. It took years to get properly diagnosed, and by the time I did, the condition had already taken over large parts of my life. Anyone who has experienced cluster headaches understands that this isn’t just a headache—it’s a cycle of severe, debilitating pain that dictates how you live day to day.
Before I found an effective system, I relied on multiple treatments just to function. I used Rizatriptan, which could take up to an hour to work. Then I moved to Sumatriptan nasal spray, which worked in about 25 minutes, and eventually injectable Sumatriptan, which was faster. I also used high-flow oxygen at home set to 15 liters per minute, and I carried a portable unit at work. Even with all of this, my life revolved around anticipating attacks—how many injections I had left, where I could go, and whether I could even leave the house.
Preventive therapy helped stabilize things. I currently take Verapamil daily and use Emgality monthly. These treatments made a difference, but they didn’t completely free me from the cycles.
After doing a lot of research, I came across information from groups like Clusterbusters and studies being done at Yale. I read about people using psilocybin in different ways—microdosing, moderate dosing, and high-dose “cycle-breaking” approaches. After careful consideration, I chose the high-dose method.
My first experience was a full weekend: 7 grams on Friday, 7 grams on Saturday, and 7 grams on Sunday. What I noticed immediately, the intensity was way less and they started to come back slowly, not immediate. Another surprising change was how my oxygen therapy worked. Before, it would take 15 to 20 minutes to stop an attack. After that weekend, when I have any symptoms at all, oxygen worked in about 5 minutes. That alone was life-changing.
Over the course of a year, I repeated this same three-day protocol two more times, spacing each weekend several months apart. Each time, the result was consistent: the cluster headaches stopped. When they eventually returned, they came back slower, less intense, and much more manageable.
At one point, I stopped my preventive medications to see what would happen. The cluster headaches came back. When I restarted my medications, they went away again. That confirmed for me that my baseline treatment—Verapamil and Emgality—is still essential.
What I’ve learned from this journey is that it’s not one single solution—it’s a combination. Preventive therapy keeps things stable. Acute treatments like oxygen and triptans provide backup when needed. And for me, psilocybin appeared to interrupt the cycle in a way nothing else had.
Today, my life is very different. I’m no longer trapped by my condition the way I once was. I still respect it, I still prepare for it, but I have tools that work—and that has given me a level of control I didn’t think was possible before.
This is my personal experience. It’s not a recommendation or a guideline, but it is
topic: My Journey with Episodic Cluster Headaches and Psilocybin Intervention Adam Estroff – Parkland, Florida

I’ve been an episodic cluster headache sufferer for nearly twenty years now. I am father of three with a fairly stressful job in the tech industry. I usually get a flare up of cluster headaches every 12 to 18 months. They typically last 3 months.
Last winter, the headaches were starting to come on, and I did 4 doses of magic mushrooms one week apart (around 1 gram each time). It did the trick and I experienced nearly four months of completely pain free existence, not even a hint of shadows, which is very unusual for me.
Unfortunately in the spring the headaches came back. I only had 1 gram of mushrooms left and it wasn’t enough. I never experienced a flare up in the spring so I thought I was safe for a while. I was about to make another batch… Lesson learned.
I’m now 3 and a half months into this flare up. It is starting to cool off finally. In the middle of the summer I was at 800 mg of verapamil. The side effects weren’t pleasant. Now I’m down to 480. I plan on going fully verapamil free in the next three weeks. Five days after being off verapamil, I plan on taking 2 grams of mushroom as a precaution, and repeat the dose every two or three weeks for the foreseeable future.

I felt compelled to share my story after seeing Bob Wold present at Horizons 2009 tonight on vimeo. This is my first post. I welcome your comments. Please forgive me if I have inadvertently violated any forum rules or guidelines, or if I am repeating common knowledge.
NB: I am not a doctor. I am an opinionated total stranger writing under a pseudonym. Nothing I am saying could or should be construed as medical advice.
I started getting severe headaches when I was very young. One day, when I was about five, my parents gave me the usual 2 acetaminophen and sent me to bed. About 20 minutes later, I came out of my room in tears. Exasperated, they wanted to know why. I told them because I couldn't handle the pain, and that if felt like someone was stabbing me in the eyes. Off to the doctor we went.
There was never really an answer to the headache question. At first, I was prescribed Fioricet (acetaminophen+butalbital+caffeine).  The headaches were called tension, then migraine, then cluster, then migraine. I was more interested in ending the pain then having an accurate label.
I do also get normal headaches, which are a totally different beast. No eye pain, no light sensitivity. I pop 2 aspirin+acetaminophen+caffeine and go about my day. If they get bad but not really bad, my favorite self-remedy is standing in a warm shower in a dark bathroom with my face directly under the spray until it stops. I also do this during really bad ones.
Some of the worse headaches were the ones that woke me up. I would usually be somewhat confused; and I would often spend a while in bed in terrible pain before I would get up and take pills. Sometimes, the pain would be entangled in a dream, and I would think if I could solve a math problem, or dial a phone, or repot a plant (I can remember those three dreams to this day, and they happened between 10 and 25 years ago), the pain would stop.
Later in life, they switched me to Zomig (zolmitriptan). The Zomig works ~75% of the time. When it doesn't work, it makes the pain worse. Currently, my insurance won't pay for Zomig, and so I have sumatriptan (Imitrex), which I haven't had to try yet, as I have one Zomig left.
When I was about 19, I entered another stage. The headaches became more common and lasted longer. The pain was worse.
I distinctly remember the worse one I ever had (and I bet you do, too.) It was 1999, and I was 21. The pain started when I was at work, about 6 hours from the end of my shift. Stupidly, I had no pills with me. I was already in trouble over attendance, so I wanted to tough it out. I sat at my computer, and the pain got worse and worse. Finally, with 15 minutes left in my shift, I had to leave immediately, or I was going to vomit all over my cubicle. They said they would have to write me up. I said I didn't care.
I drove 115 miles per hour down the highway to get home. I do not normally drive above 75. I knew I might hit and kill someone. I didn't care. I was crying. I was shifting the position of m

It was impossible not to cry when I started reading many of your messages.Â  Crying is something I pay dearly for, I can already feel the tips of my fingers turning into icicles and I know what's coming next.Â
Here's my story...
I'm am pretty sure I was born with a migraine.Â  My mother said I cried for 6 months straight.Â  (maybe it was my destiny, maybe it was because I was a high forcep delivery, I did have bruises on my temples for 6 weeks my mom said) She said one day I just stopped crying and started talking! At 7 months old.Â  I was reading and writing by 3.Â  Then, my first memories of headaches started at about 4.Â  I remember burying my head in my couch and in the corner of the back seat of my car.Â  I threw up all the time.Â  And it took 2 days to recover each time.Â  I missed school weekly from kindergarten to 3rd grade.Â  At the age of 6 they hospitalized me for a week to do all sorts of testing.Â  The conclusion was migraines. They had me on phenobarbital for a long time.Â  I think close to a year.Â  Then at 7 they very very slowly weened me off. I was headache free from ages 8 to 11.Â
Two things happened at 11.Â  We moved from Vermont to Tennessee.Â  And, I started getting my pre-menstrual hormones.Â  I have two type A parents and I was constantly called lazy in the summer and once a month when I got my period.Â  I just couldn't function.Â  But I tried to just push through.Â  I started popping exedrin like candy as soon as I could buy them myself at 14.Â  Then we moved to Colorado, and the heat was never that bad.Â  And it always cooled down at night.Â  So, I just got used to having really bad migraines one week a month, on hot days, whenever I cried and when big storm pressures came in.Â  I just took exedrin and imitrex and managed.Â  That was my life.Â  "The good ole days" I call them.Â  And once I was on my own I didn't get called names for taking it easy when I had headaches. And I should mention every single time I drank a lick of alcohol, I pay and suffer the most, for days, so I stopped that a long time ago. I can't even smell it anymore without triggering a headache.
Now, this is important to the study you are doing.Â  At 21, my parents took my kids for the weekend and I had my first magic mushroom experience.Â  I loved it.Â  I loved it so much that I left my kids with family once or twice a year and experienced it throughout my entire 20's.Â  During my 20's, the migraines were very few and far between.Â  I thought I had "outgrown" the severe, daily headaches and thought I outgrew being so sensitive to everything.Â  I never considered that the mushrooms had anything to do with it.

This is a post about holistic health and does not directly relate to mushrooms etc.Â  While it is by no means necessary to be in good health to embark on mushroom therapy I personally believe that it helps.Â  I also believe that good health has a positive impact on the condition of CH itself.Â  I believe this because I've experience poor heath, normal health, and good health and the severity and duration of my episodes corresponded to the overall state of my health.Â  Â This is just a hunch, nothing more.
Between 2002 & 2006 I pretty much ruined my health with alcoholism.Â  Aged 36 I could't even climb a flight of stairs without getting breathless.Â  Picking up the pieces in 2006 my goal was to reverse the damage, and that led to an interesting journey of discovery.Â  The result has been a massive improvement in my health and general well being.Â  Let me put this in perspective.Â  I suffered a serious back injury and an MRI revealed that I'd herniated 4 discs.Â  The consultant told me that I'd never lift weights again.Â  I took to drinking, increasing my weekly consumption to 150-200 units of alcohol.Â  Four years of my life pretty much passed me by.Â  It ruined my marriage and it ruined my health.Â  Now, not only can I run up 14 flights of stairs, I can crank out 30+ consecutive handstand pushups on a good day.Â  Everything can be repaired if you go about it the right way.
AlsoÂ… entheogenic substances produce a body load and this is much easier to tolerate if you are healthy and have a general feeling of well being going into the treatment.Â  It all makes for an easier trip.
One thing that I've learned is that there is a significant difference between what people generally perceive to be good health, and what really is good health!Â  Over the course of the past 4 years I've tested out dozens of theories regarding exercise and diet on my own body, and though many of them have brought minor and cumulative improvements, it wasn't until the past 12 months that the final pieces clicked into place.Â  SInce then I've passed this information onto friends and family, and it's made a dramatic difference to several lives.
This may seem daunting, but it's not hard to make and maintain the necessary lifestyle changes.Â  One step at a time, one day at a time.Â  Pretty much anyone can completely transform their wellbeing in a year with demonstrable improvements within a week.Â  The absolute key to all this is that it's a holistic approach and missing out one component will not produce anything approaching the full potential.Â  The whole is much greater than the sum of the parts.Â  Having said that if you only do one thing then add fresh vegetable juice to your diet

First of all, I always knew you fellow cluster headache sufferers were out there I know you were advocating for CH to be recognized properly, I was apart of a CH group on Facebook for awhile.
I have been suffering from the condition for about 8 years now, the first 4 years the Doctors had zero clue what was wrong with me, I'm episodic, usually when the season changes, winter-spring, fall-winter, never have gotten a cycle twice in one year, so i'm lucky and only get one cycle. My remission periods last from 1-2 years, so in the past 8 years I have been thru maybe 6 cycles, currently on my 6th, a little over half way on current cylce (I hope). My cycles will last 2-3 months, current cycle i'm on now, I am about 2 months deep into it on August 1st. When the pain gets real bad, I take a little drive down to my local hospital and visit the Emergency Room, most of the nurses know me and are familiar with me now haha. I get put into a room fairly quick, they seem to understand that i'm dealing with some major pain and I would only go there if I couldn't handle it at home. I am given Toradol and Maxeran, one for pain and one for nausea, than they give me some oxygen for awhile. I think its safe to say that most things don't work and this is the case i'm in most of my visits to the local ER haha, sometimes it helps, but most of the time it doesn't...I think I just like knowing that they are there to help and just going thru the process of it all, kind of takes my focus away from this horrible pain I have on the right side of my head and eye.
I've been given all sorts of stuff earlier on into my headaches, anti-depressants to "shock the brain" lol, that was funny when the DR told me what he wanted me to take them for. A great deal of pain-killers and anxiety medications, they really liked to try me on different anti-depressants though. I was young and had half a brain, this shit wasn't working, so I would stop taking these medications cause it was doing absolutely nothing for me in terms on headaches and the pain. Finally, after one lucky visit to ER, the DR there seen my record and how many visits i've been having and FINALLY secured myself a referral to see a Neurologist. So came on the xrays of my sinuses (nothing) of my neck (slight arthritis but still nothing out of the ordinary) CT Scans of the ol'noggin (the usual stuff is there :)) and i'm off to see the Neuro. He puts me on anti-seizure medication (Valproic Acid) and nasal spray (triptans, which by the way, seem to help, nasal spray that is), only crappy part is, that was my last cycle two years ago and by the time he gave me the prescription, I was out of cycle already lol.

This is the first time I have posted here.   I have been visiting the clusterbusters site off an on for the past couple of years - but I have never felt compelled to post anything here until now.  Every time I read a post where someone describes what they are going through I can relate.  Some of the stories here are really sad - and I hope these people take the busters' advice because its all good and has helped me immensely.  I have been reduced to tears reading many of the stories because I know what it is like, and I have had help and I am finally making progress and I there might be some light at the end of this very dark tunnel I have been living in for years,
I have been dealing with CH now for 13 years.  For the first couple of years I was told that I had a a severe case of Sinusitis and that was what was causing my pain.  The doc would give me some Avamys or some other nasal cortosoid, some antibiotics and send me on my way.  This 'worked' for a while as my clusters only lasted for a couple of  weeks and I figured that the sinus infection had passed and I was fine again.  This happened two to three times a year.  No biggie.   The attacks became worse and less tolerable as time went on.   I started to suspect that I did not have sinusitis - even though I felt like I had been kicked in the face and my sinuses were plugged.  At the time I did not have a regular doctor and if I showed up at the hospital or urgent care facility suffering from a CH I was often dismissed as 'drug seeking' and sent home, because the time that any doctor decided to see me the CH had passed and I was feeling 'normal' again.  I did eventually find a doctor who diagnosed me with CH, but he did not know too much about it and treated it like a standard migraine.   Maxalt as an abortive and amytripaline as a preventative - that didn't work, but at least I had a name for what I was dealing with now.  10 years ago the internet was not what it is now.  Cluster Headaches were less known and there were virtually no options for people.  My CH was episodic for 8 years - then they starting getting worse.  The cycles got longer and more frequent, until I got to the point where I am now.   Chronic CH - 4-6 times a day for the past  five years.  I get my 'breaks' from time to time where I will experience a one or two days CH free and I have good days (1 or 2) and I have really bad days (6+ attacks per day).
About three years ago I was fortunate enough to be put in touch with the CHAMP (Calgary Headache Assessment and Management Program) in Calgary.   The team of docs there are nothing short of amazing.  I have had MRI's done, I got a prescription for oxygen (a bit of a struggle to get initially from GP - CHAMP said NO PROBLEM), I got a scrip of Sumitriptan (Immitrex) and they started trying out different preventative meds.   I tried Verapimil.   It didn't really help me, I kept going for my ECG's and all was well until I hit the effective dosage and then BAM the worst tension head

Hi to all.. a quick disclaimer: THIS IS LONG and I apologize. I've considered posting for years, but I'm so very much not inclined to "put myself out there" - maybe a bit of a technophobe.. but i've never chatted, instant messaged, facebooked, etc.Â  I have looked at the well known clusterheadache sites for years, found cluster busters a few years back,Â  and finally decided it's time to share.Â  I think one of the most frustrating things for me as a cluster headache sufferer is the fact that so few people have ever even heard of them, and every time i try to explain to people why i'm clutching my eye, or "am on the wagon" or have oxygen cylinders all over my house, i get the feeling that people just think i need to go get my little tin foil hat and put it on.. if you know what i mean....Â  I want to say to the "community" here that it is very inspiring the way everyone supports one another, and over the years just reading the posts has been so helpful to make me feel like i'm not alone. Others who understand how much this disease can rob your joy.Â  Â  So, thanks to all that have gone before me. I'm glad I found you and that folks were willing to put in the work to create this resource.
Background on me -Â  I'm a male 41 who first began having clusters around 19 or 20 yrs of age. Episodic sufferer now for last 15 years. Usually have one cycle a year, lasting 2-3 months.Â  They started randomly. Would get individual attacks - kip 3-6 with no real pattern. I wonder now if what kept the beast at bay for those first couple of years was the fact that on a few occasions in college (literally 3-4 times) i did small recreational doses of shrooms.Â  I was never a big fan of the idea of "seeing something that wasn't there" so i was very leary.. err.. paranoid of the whole hallucinogen thing - just wasn't my "thing".Â  I liked that i could try shrooms in small doses but when i tried them I always did enough to "feel" it but nothing too intense.Â  When i first read about busting, i was happy to read that you can have success with smaller doses than what is considered "recreational".Â  Â So, back to history..Â  I was around 25 when i had my first real cycle.Â  After about a week of 3 to 5 hits a day of what i used to call "my eye headaches" I finally knuckled under and headed for the family doc... convinced of my upcoming brain scan and diagnosis of brain tumor, aneurism, etc.Â  Doc listened to my symptoms and casually said, 'oh, you have cluster headaches'. Posted here with the authors blessing.
What is 5-MeO-DALT, and why should you care?
The short version is that 5-MeO-DALT is a fairly new option for cluster headache patients who are comfortable self-administering treatments. It is a simple tryptamine that acts on serotonin receptors in the brain. When used carefully, 5-MeO-DALT is extremely safe and is proving to be very effective for some cluster headache patients.
Now, the FAQs:
Q: Who invented this cluster headache treatment?
A: Nobody invented it as a cluster headache treatment at all. The Â“recipeÂ” was devised by Alexander Shulgin in 2004, and later synthesized by a chemist. It became a relatively obscure Â“research chemicalÂ” used rarely as a recreational drug. More on that later. I stumbled upon 5-MeO-DALT because I was looking for a molecule that had some of the good properties of the things we know, like psilocybin and LSA (stopping my cluster headaches) without the bad properties (psychedelic trips, nausea, jail time).
Q: How does it work?
A: 5-MeO-DALT is a serotonergic. It works by engaging our serotonin receptors and sending a signal to our brains. The whole mechanism of exactly how this stops cluster headaches is still a mystery, but there are two key components: affinity and signaling. Affinity is how well a molecule Â“sticksÂ” to the right receptors, and signaling is the actual message that it sends to our brains. 5-MeO-DALT has a higher affinity than psilocybin for the 5HT2A receptor, and it seems to send the right signal.
Q: What makes it better?
A: IÂ’m not willing to say that itÂ’s Â“betterÂ” than other alternative treatments, but I will point out the advantages. One advantage is dosage control. When you take 15mg of 5-MeO-DALT, you are getting 15mg of 5-MeO-DALT. By comparison, when you take an extraction of rivea corymbosa seeds, you are getting an unknown amount of LSA along with hundreds of other chemicals, some of them volatile. When you take 1g of mushrooms, you are getting an unknown amount of psilocybin along with hundreds of other chemicals, some of them volatile. Why is this important? Imagine listening to your favorite song. Now imagine listening to your favorite song while vacuuming, the dog is barking, a baby is crying, and someone is ringing your doorbell. Same song? Not really. Every chemical you put in your body has a ripple effect. 5-MeO-DALT is a single molecule that does one thing.
Secondly, the side effects are almost non-existent. Many of us avoid using tryptamines over fears of a Â“tripÂ”. With a normal dose of 15mg of 5-MeO-DALT, most people notice absolutely no side effects at all. It has an extremely short half-life, so even if the user feels some effects (cold and sleepy are the two most common), it is out of your system in two hours.
Finally, it is very user-friendly. Accurate dosing of 5-MeO-DALT requires only a decent milligram scale and some empty gel capsules. For those of us who donÂ’t have the patience to grow anything or do our own extraction

This is a long babble, but thereÂ’s some good info here for the noobs.Â  Keep in mind we're all different.Â  This is just what works for me.
IÂ’ve been using the Cluster Buster method since Â’09.Â  I just went to the Clusterheadaches blog and was hoping to get an answer to a questionÂ… Â“Can a vaccine trigger a cluster period?Â”Â  What I got felt like a gang rape with everyone screaming D3 with no one really answering my question.Â
So on top of the information IÂ’ll post here, if youÂ’ve had a vaccine and it triggered a CP let me knowÂ…Â  And if youÂ’ve had a vaccine and it didnÂ’t trigger a CP also let me know.Â  IÂ’d appreciate it.
HereÂ’s the background I added to that question:
I finally got a correct diagnosis in Â’04.Â  My headache log proved to be invaluable in making the diagnosis.Â  From Â’04-Â‘09 I tried everything in my neurologistÂ’s play book and nothing worked or worked very well.Â  To be fair I did get some relief from time to time, but as we all know the beast changes and what works today may not work tomorrow.
After an 11 month cluster period that spanned Â’08-Â’09, I did some research and found the Harvard Medical School research paper titled Â“Psychedelics in the Treatment of Cluster HeadacheÂ”.Â  I had pretty much gone chronic and I couldnÂ’t handle much more pain (my gun was looking like a real option) so with nothing to lose and all to gain I tried the cluster buster method.Â
In Â’09 I found that a light giggly buzz from a little mushroom would give me two weeks pain free.Â  After 11 months of pain I was like a dog off its leash.Â  Once I would get another attack, I would repeat the process and get yet another two weeks pain free.Â  Lather, rinse repeat.
So every two weeks or so Alice and I would go down the rabbit hole and kick the beastÂ’s ass, or at least knock him out for a while.
This went on till I successfully broke the cycle or I went into remission.Â  IÂ’m not so sure which it was.Â  I havenÂ’t had an attack since early Â’12 and (due to fear of them coming back), I medicate about once a month.Â  IÂ’ve been working on cutting that back to every three months.Â  I think of it as a booster shot.
I wonÂ’t use that 4-letter word (cure) here.Â  What I will say is thisÂ… A little mushroom helped me regain my life.Â  ItÂ’s the best preventative medication IÂ’ve found to-date and IÂ’ve tried a bunch of them.
The beast is different for all of us although our common bond is the pain and disruption of our lives we all experience as CH sufferers.Â  What works for me may not work for you although I can say for sure, oxygen is your best friend.Â  Ice is nice and caffeine can help.

Somewhere during this time the headaches start every day at 11am, 2pm, and 5pm.
Try everything from Tylenol to ibuprofen find out ONLY Excedrin Migraine works.
Start taking it at the onset of a headache. At 5pm - if the Excedrin worked i was headache free the remainder of the night. If it failed to work i kept the headache until 11am the next morning.

only taking Excedrin Migraine every morning and every time a headache comes around. Hello all.  I wanted to share a little of my story with clusters.  I should have probably posted this a little while back, but its hard to sometimes relive all of it. I apologize for how long this is.  I should work on a cliff notes version.
Everything started in January of 2014.  I started waking up during sleep every hour or two with the most intense pain i had ever felt in and around my right eye.  The pain shot through my head and went down the right side of my neck and last about 30 minutes to an hour.  At first I figured I must be sleeping wrong.  First I tried new pillows, but nothing changed.  Then I thought maybe a chiropractor could help, but was of course wrong.  The pain kept hitting me every night, and consistently every 2 hours after I went to sleep.  Finally went to a doctor after a month.  Up to this point the headaches only came on at night.  During the day everything was fine except me being a little exhausted.  The doctor didn't really have any ideas and wanted me to go to physical therapy.  I knew he was wrong and never did go.  Started looking for a new doctor after that.
The new doctor was much more helpful, but still didn't have any ideas.  Then one night we had a breakthrough that helped pinpoint the problem.  This is something that is hard to explain.  One night shortly after my first visit with the new doctor I was in the kitchen trying to ride one of the headaches out.  There was a voice in my head.  It just said take the medicine in the junk drawer.  My wife used to suffer from migraines, but they stopped about 6 months before my clusters.  Unknown to me one of her old maxalt tabs was in that drawer.  I took it and the headache melted away within 5 minutes.  It never came back that night.
With that new information and a little googling my wife and I came across cluster headaches.  All the symptoms lined up exactly with what I had been dealing with.  On the next visit I explained all this to the new doc.  She said she had also done some research and wanted to talk to me about cluster headaches.  This was when I was given my first prescription for verapamil.
The verapamil helped, but about this time my clusters also changed.  Instead of just getting them at night I started getting them during the day.  I also started experiencing my first shadows about this time.  Headaches were always about the same time.  The cycles would drift some.  Looser schedule and less intense, but slowly becoming fixed at the same times each day and much more intense.  Then the cycle would fade and start over.  During this time I was getting the headaches pretty much every day.  There was the occasional random day where I was pain free, but it was rare.

Hello everyone, this is my first post as I am new to the community. I’ll first share my story trying to be brief (I won’t/can't).
I’ve separated my post in a first presentation part, and in a second part where i talk about my recent busting experience. English is not my native language but don't hesitate to correct me if I am gramatically wrong.
My name is Serge, 32 years old, from Geneva in Switzerland. I am born from a Romanian father and a Guatemalan mother. I’ve been in couple for more than 10 years and married for almost 5. I have two boys 5 and 6 years old. I’ve had headaches from as long as I could remember, but i couldn’t identify patterns. They were just so bad and no doctor was able to understand it.
My cluster journey really started in september 2018, we went to spain in vacation and everyday I got terrible headaches. When we got back home, they were becoming worse, showing up at night, at work, it started having a really bad impact on my life. After two years of suffering almost everyday, i wasn’t strong enough to keep my job.
I was a gate and check-in supervisor at the local airport, I had a good carreer there on the rise, but it came to a stop. In this job, you need to be fully available, you work early and late, you can't be absent even for a few minutes if you're in pain, so it wasnt compatible with my shit condition.
I’ve kept trying to go back to work after some resting periods, and it never worked, the intense stress, hard schedules, made my pain worsening when back at work.
When you are on sick leave for more than 3 months, the swiss law requires people to apply for disability. therefore, during a very long process, I’ve been sent to make some medical and psychologicyl exams to definite if I can still work or not.
It was around the same time that a proper cluster headache diagnosis had been made. Before this i was suggested (and convinced) that I had arnold neuralgia, I’ve already had tried all the numerous migraine medications you know.I had already tried nerve decompression in the back of the head too, with no results of course.
In late 2020 the examinations i underwent concluded that i had borderline personality disorders, and confirmed the cluster headache diagnosis.
At this moment when I had this annoucement I was trying to work again at the airport, but it didn’t went well, my wife and I lost our 5 weeks old daughter and that sent me into an even deeper depression, leading to the end of my contract.

Greetings!
Hello folks. I have been lingering around this board and the clusterheadaches.com board since 2001. It was at that time, when I was 16 years old, I was diagnosed with clusterheadache. It is hard to imagine that soon I will have had these things for half of my lifetime, and have still not found the best treatment for me. I guess it is just an evolution. I hope to use this thread to detail my work thus far to combat my current cycle, as well as the work I still have ahead of me. Feel free to comment, questions, follow along, whatever you'd like.
My Background Previous to Current Cycle: (Feel free to skip over)
I had what I thought were migraines only ever once in a while (one every few years) as a young child. Didn't think a whole lot about it. Then I had them three days in a row in 2001 at the exact same time each day and thought "Holy shit". I thought I was going to die. I was fortunate enough to receive a CH diagnosis right then from my family practitioner. He prescribed a prednisone taper, Inderal, and Zomig. The Prednisone and the inderal killed the cycle.
